coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence and solid-state LED application based on pH-sensitive carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yuchan
Zeng, Fujia
Zhang, Liangrui
Chen, Guanghao
Zhang, Qian
Zhang, Nan
Zhang, Wenxia
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great interest on account of their outstanding physicochemical properties. Herein we study the optical properties of the CQDs (diamond structure) and observe interesting phenomenon of concurrent multiband luminescence across the whole visible region. What is more, the CQDs exhibit emission-dependent excitation and high pH-sensitive. The surface structural and luminescence investigation in conjunction with density functional theory calculation reveal that C(O)O, C(O)NH2, and C(O)OH-related surface defects in these CQDs lead to observed luminescence. The calculation shows that stronger transitions between band edges of C(O)NH2-terminated CQDs leads to relatively strong photoluminescence in strong contrast with usually weak emission in oxygen terminated CQDs. Finally, the solid-state LED devices with different emission colors were fabricated based on the pH-sensitive properties of the CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence origin
Surface defects
pH-sensitive

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) TEM images of diamond QDs. (c) the inset of that high-resolution TEM images. (d) Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of diamond QDs. (e) XRD spectrum of diamond QDs. (f) Raman spectrum of diamond QDs.

PL spectra of diamond QDs aqueous solutions: (a) as-prepared, (b) HCl-treated, (c) NaOH-treated, and (d) HCl + NaOH co-treated diamond QDs. Plot of (e) intensity at emission maximum and (f) intensity of peaks at 460, 480, and 510 nm versus pH values.
(a) FTIR spectra of as-prepared, HCl-treated, and NaOH-treated diamond QDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s and (c) N 1 s for as-prepared, HCl-treated, and NaOH-treated diamond QDs.
(top) Three configurations of diamond QDs bonded with different surface functional groups used for DFT calculation. (bottom) Corresponding transition matrix elements.
Electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the solid-state LED based on (a) as-prepared and (b) HCl-treated diamond QDs.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence and solid-state LED application based on pH-sensitive carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing 400065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Optoelectronic Engineering, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing 400065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great interest on account of their outstanding physicochemical properties. Herein we study the optical properties of the CQDs (diamond structure) and observe interesting phenomenon of concurrent multiband luminescence across the whole visible region. What is more, the CQDs exhibit emission-dependent excitation and high pH-sensitive. The surface structural and luminescence investigation in conjunction with density functional theory calculation reveal that C(O)O, C(O)NH2, and C(O)OH-related surface defects in these CQDs lead to observed luminescence. The calculation shows that stronger transitions between band edges of C(O)NH2-terminated CQDs leads to relatively strong photoluminescence in strong contrast with usually weak emission in oxygen terminated CQDs. Finally, the solid-state LED devices with different emission colors were fabricated based on the pH-sensitive properties of the CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging member of carbon family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were discovered serendipitously by Xu et al. purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes fabricated by arc-discharge methods [1]. On account of their environmentally friendly nature, hypotoxicity, emission tunability [2–7], and good biocompatibility [8–11], CQDs have attracted tremendous interest in the last nineteen years. All the time, CQDs have been widely applications in bioimaging [8,12–14], fluorescent probes [11,15–18], photocatalysis [19,20], solar cells [21,22] and solid-state light emitting device (LED) [7,23–26]. Typically, the hexagon-CQDs-based LEDs show a high external quantum efficiency of 4.6 % and a record maximum brightness of 11,615 cd m−2 [27]; More recently, CQDs have enabled high efficiency luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) [28–30], and so far the power conversion efficiency of the large-area CQDs-integrated tandem LSCs reaches up to 6.78 % [28]. And beyond that, CQDs composites with long-lived afterglow and ultra-high afterglow quantum yields of 193.5 ms and 35.07 % were obtained by Gao et al. in 2023 [31].

Usually, CQDs include graphite quantum dots (GQDs), diamond quantum dots (diamond QDs), and the scarce quantum dots (C8-structured QDs). It is worth noting that the latter two quantum dots belong to the superhard nanostructure. The diamond QDs generally consist of about 98 % carbon with residual hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen, possess a sp3 hybridized core, and have small amount of graphitic carbon on the surface. Notably, these carbon sources do not contain benzene rings, such as glucose [32], sucrose [33], and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) [34]. Besides this, the strong acidic synthetic environment tends to produce diamond structure, and the weak acidic or strong alkaline is more conductive to the formation of C8 structure. Thus, the raw materials (without benzene rings) and the hydrothermal environment provided by strong alkalinity or acids should be the two main factors for the chemical mechanism of diamond structure formation. Among them, the diamond QDs represent a truly unique testbed for the study the fluorescence origin of CQDs. The preparation methods for diamond QDs are usually characterized by dangerous, time-consuming, uneconomical, and complicated, such as detonation of carbon-containing explosives [35], chemical vapour deposition (CVD) [36], high-energy ball milling of diamond microcrystals [37], and laser ablation [38], etc. In contrast, although it is difficult to purify and dry quantum dots prepared by hydrothermal method, which is relatively simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly.

The photoluminescence (PL) from CQDs is mostly attributed to recombination with a surface-related origin rather than the interband transition from sp2/sp3 carbon skeleton [39–41]. Due to the diversity and complexity of surface defect states, the fluorescence behavior varies from emission-dependent excitation, emission-independent excitation to multicolor emission. Although there has been a lot of significant research on CQDs, the origin of luminescence in CQDs remains a big mystery and highly debated. Herein, we develop one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize diamond QDs, which is a special carrier for studying the luminescence mechanism on account of the characteristics of wide band gap. Significantly, the prepared diamond QDs exhibit a series of emission bands across the whole visible region, and highly pH-sensitive fluorescence characteristics. Finally, the diamond QDs were used as light-converting phosphors materials to fabricate solid-state LED. We aim to address the underlying luminescence mechanism behind this enticing phenomenon via experimental study combining with theoretical calculation.

The diamond QDs were synthesized with ammonium citrate and ammonium bicarbonate. The mixed solution of 3 g ammonium citrate, 1 g ammonium bicarbonate and 20 ml deionized water underwent ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The mixed solution reacted at 180 °C for 10 h in a teflon lined stainless steel autoclave placed in a drying oven, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained solution underwent centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant containing diamond QDs was separated out and divided into eight equal shares. One share served ae as-prepared diamond QDs with pH equal to 7. An appropriate amount of HCl was added into the second, third and fourth share, and the pH values were adjusted to 3,4 and 5, respectively. The pH of the fifth, sixth, and seventh share were tuned to 9, 10 and 11 by adding right amount of NaOH, respectively. Finally, for the eighth share, HCl was first added to tune the pH to 3, and then NaOH was added to adjust the pH to 7. The concentrations of the diamond nanocrystal solutions in each group were tuned to be identical through dilution.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a transmission electron microscope (Talos F200S). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed by using a powder diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (1.5406 Å, 36 kV, 20 mA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on the Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra, and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum were acquired using the Edinburgh FLS1000. The quantum yield (QY) of the diamond QDs was measured by using quinine sulfate in a 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution (QY = 54 %) as the reference sample. The QY of diamond QDs was calculated by comparing integrated photoluminescence intensity (excited at 340 nm) and absorbance (less than 0.1 at 340 nm) of the sample with those of quinine sulfate.

The calculation was performed with the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP 5.4.4). Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) formulation of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was adopted to treat electron exchange and correlation. The kinetic energy cutoff of the plane wave basis set was set to 400 eV. The convergence criterion for electronic relaxations was 10–5 eV, and the force criterion for structural optimization was set to 0.01 eV/Å. The 1 × 1 × 1 Gamma scheme k-point mesh were used to sample the first Brillouin zone for quantum dots model.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) method was used to characterize the as prepared diamond QDs. It can be clearly seen that the quasi-spherical diamond QDs are well dispersed (Fig. 1a to 1b). The lattice fringes of the nanoparticles are clear in Fig. 1c, suggesting that they are single crystals. The lattice spacing is about 2.04 Å, which is consistent with that of the (111) plane of the cubic diamond [42,43]. Fig. 1d is the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image of the diamond QDs, and the calculated lattice spacing values (Table S2) are also well consistent with those of the diamond [42]. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1e) of shows a broad peak at approximately 32.30, which attributed to some amorphous carbon coated diamond QDs, the oxygen containing functional groups, and the (110) crystal planes of diamond [42,43]. In addition, a shoulder peak at 440 that can be assigned to the (111) lattice plane of a cubic diamond [42], which is consistent with the HR-TEM and SAED results. Notably, owing to the small dimensions of diamond QDs and existence of surplus organic films. The peaks are wide and overlap with each. The diamond peak can be seen at 1333 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 1f), the peak at 1578 cm−1 mostly attributed to the diamond-like signal and may also partially arise from the G-band of graphitic carbon [44]. These results suggested that the synthesized nanoparticles are diamond structure.

The diamond QDs show high pH-sensitive, lager full width at half maximum (FWHM) of about 88 nm, and emission-dependent excitation optical behaviors (Fig. 2a–d). The PL spectra of the as-prepared, HCl-treatment, NaOH treatment, HCl and NaOH co-treatment samples all comprise three distinct emission bands: two blue bands centered at 460 and 480 nm, and a green band at 510 nm. Obviously, the emission variation of alkaline and neutral samples (as-prepared and HCl-NaOH co-treated) is very similar, that is, the emission intensity at 460 nm is the strongest, emission at about 480 nm is the secondly, and the green band at around 510 nm is the weakest. Conversely, for the acidic sample treated by HCl, the intensity of emission band at 460 nm is the weakest, the 510 nm is secondly, the peak at 480 nm becomes the strongest. From the Fig. 2e, we can see that the intensity at emission maximum of alkaline sample is obviously stronger than that of the acid sample, which is consistent with that the fluorescence quantum yields of alkaline samples (about 45.7 %) are higher than that of acidic samples (about 5.3 %). Specifically, the non-radiative transition caused by the large specific surface area, oxygen-containing functional groups and internal defects often leads to the low quantum yield of the diamond QDs. In addition, we did not passivate the surface of diamond QDs here, so the quantum yield is relatively low. Fig. 2f displays the intensity of the emission peaks at 460, 480 and 510 nm. Significantly, the luminescence intensity of the three emission bands showed three different change laws with pH values. The 460-nm blue band is strongly pH dependent, that is, the emission intensity is weakest when in acidic condition and strongest in alkaline, which is completely opposite to the variation laws of pH-sensitive 480-nm emission. Only the emission band at 510 nm is pH-insensitive. At a glance, the intensity of 480-nm emission decreases slowly with pH value, while that for 510-nm emission band increases slowly with pH value. But in fact, as the strongest 460-nm emission for the alkaline sample, the wider FWHM have a certain superposition contribution to the 480 and 510 nm emission. Thus, the intensity of 480-nm should decay faster than visually with pH, while that for 510-nm should enhance more slowly even almost remains constant. That is to say, the emission at 480 nm shows pH sensitivity and the peak at 510 nm is pH independent.

In addition, it is worth noting that the emission for the excitation wavelength between 280 and 380 nm also shows high pH dependent (Fig. S1). The variation of 450 and 472-nm emission band are the same as those of 460 and 480 nm, respectively, implying the 450 and 460 nm emission originate from the same type of surface-defect, the emission bands at 472 and 480 nm arises from a second-type surface defect, and the 510-nm emission band stem from another type of surface defect. The PL excitation (PLE) spectra of diamond QDs by different treatments are shown in Fig. S2. Comparing the variation law of emission and excitation bands, we conclude that the excitation band at 280, 400, and 423 nm correspond separately to the emission bands at 450/460, 472/480, and 510 nm. The remarkable variation of the three excitation/emission bands after surface treatments prove that they all origin from surface defect states rather than from intrinsic interband transition.

Obviously, the different surface defects are responsible for the pH-sensitive optical characteristics. Therefore, Fourier transform infrared absorption spectrum (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are used to shed new light on the luminescence origins. Fig. 3a displays the infrared absorption spectra for as-prepared, NaOH-treated, and HCl-treated diamond QDs. From the figure, we can see clearly that the as-prepared and NaOH-treated sample share the same absorption characteristic peaks. Significantly, the characteristic absorption peaks of C(O)NH2 are present in the infrared spectrum and they are: CN vibration at 1380 cm−1, NH vibration at 1580 and 3180 cm−1, CO vibration at 1677 cm−1 [45]. Besides, the absorption peaks at around 1400, 1636, and 3467 cm−1 are mostly attributed to the vibrations of the residual water molecules in the KBr pellet [46]. But the absorption at 1636 cm−1 may also partially arise from the CC stretching vibration [46]. For the HCl-treated diamond QDs, the HCl treatment largely changes the surface structure: C(O)NH2-related signals nearly vanish, while the COOH signals become evident. This variation is consistent with the observed diminishing of the 460-nm emission band and becoming prominent of the 480-nm emission band. Thus, the C(O)NH2 group acts as the surface trap leading to the strong light emission at about 460 nm, and the relatively weak emission at about 480 and 510 nm origin from the C(O)OH and C(O)O-related surface state, respectively.

The XPS characterization gives consistent conclusion with respect to the infrared absorption study. Fig. S3 shows the full-band XPS spectra of the three samples. The C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s signals are strong in the as-prepared, HCl-treated, and the HCl + NaOH co-treatment diamond QDs. Fig. 3b displays the high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s. For the as-prepared diamond QDs, the distinct peak at 284.6 eV is assigned to H–C*(=O), the C*C(O)NH2 signal at 285.5 eV [45], and the C*(O)O signal at 288.5 eV [47]. After HCl treatment, the H–C*(=O) at 284.6 eV is still existing, the C*(O)OH at 288.5 eV appears, and the C*C(O)NH2 diminishes, meanwhile, the peak at 285.5 eV C*H3 become obvious. After co-treatment by HCl and NaOH, a C-N/C*C(O)O signal at 284.5 eV [48,49] and the C*(O)O signal at 289.1 eV can be seen. The signals for oxygen (Fig. S4) are consistent with that for carbon. The signal of C(O*)O is present in all three samples. After treated by HCl, the C(O*)NH2 in as-prepared diamond QDs disappears and the signal of C(O*)OH become prominent. Besides, the peak at 535.7 eV is assigned to C–O*–C. Fig. 3c displays the spectrum of N 1 s. The strong signal at 399.7 eV and 401.4 eV are ascribed to C(O)N*H2/N*H3 and C=N/N-O for as-prepared diamond QDs, respectively. After HCl treatment, it can be found that the C=N/N-O signal at 401.3 eV become obvious, while the signal of C(O)N*H2 disappear and is substituted by N*H3. Only the C(O)N*H2 signal at 399.1 eV is found after co-treatment by HCl and NaOH [50]. Therefore, the variation law of the various XPS signals after surface treatments of the diamond QDs further confirm the above deduction on the origin of each emission band in the diamond QDs. In addition, there is an identifiable peak with a shoulder in the zeta potential of diamond QDs (Fig.S5). Due to the presence of the -C(O)O and -NH2 groups, the zeta potential was positive and negative, respectively. The result of zeta potential is consistent with that of FTIR and XPS.

To further clarify the roles of C(O)OH, C(O)O, and C(O)NH2-related surface defects in luminescence of the diamond QDs, we performed DFT calculations considering various surface functional groups. Three types of diamond QDs with different surface functional groups were constructed including C(O)OH, C(O)NH2, and C(O)O, which were confirmed the existence of such groups by the infrared absorption and XPS studies as mentioned above. Usually, the transition dipole moment (TDM), is the electric dipole moment related to the transition between an initial state a and a final state b. Without added magnetic field, the TDM can be described by
P
a → b
=
φ a
r
φ b
=
iħ
E b
−
E a
∑ i
C ai
C bi
G i
, where
E a
and
E b
are correspond separately to energy eigenstates of wave functions
φ a
and
φ b
.
C a
,
C b
, and G are plane-wave superposition coefficient and reciprocal space vector with the same k vector, respectively. The transition probabilities between the topmost valence and the lowest conduction band are revealed by the calculated sum of the squares of TDM, P2, along the K-path for band-structure [51,52]. The conveniently sized unit of the TDM is the Debye, and the values of different structures can be directly compared. As displayed in Fig. 4 , the transition probabilities between the band edge are very different, suggesting that the different surface defects induce different emission intensities. Specifically, the electron transition rate in the C(O)NH2-diamond QDs is much larger than that in the C(O)O-diamond QDs or COOH-diamond QDs. Consequently, the emission intensity of C(O)NH2-diamond QDs is the strongest, C(O)O-diamond QDs is next, and COOH-diamond QDs is the weakest. This is consistent with the experiment results.

To take full advantage of the diamond QDs with low toxicity and color tunability, solid-state LED was fabricated by coating diamond QDs on the lampshades of 365 nm commercial LED. Fig. 5 and Fig. S6 show the EL spectra of the devices based on the as-prepared, HCl-treated, HCl + NaOH co-treated, and NaOH-treated diamond QDs at different operating currents. For neutral and alkaline samples, the EL spectra consist of a strong composition centering at about 530 nm, whose color temperature (CCT) is mainly between 5000 and 7000 K. For the HCl-treated sample, there is a broad emission centering at 555 nm and a weaker shoulder (632 nm) on the side of the yellow emission peak, and the CCT and CIE (Fig. S7) chromaticity coordinates is 3477 K and (0.4457, 0.4948). Evidently, the EL spectrum of the diamond QDs is much wider and redshift than their PL spectra, and this is ascribed to the following reasons. Firstly, there are opposite charges on the surface of the CQDs, the particles are easy to agglomerate, which is consistent with both positive and negative zeta potential. When the nanoparticle forms solid film, agglomeration of the particle causes the size to become larger, which leads to the red shift of their emission. Furthermore, the partial aggregation of the diamond QDs is strong and the crystal fusion may occur at their interfaces, leading to formation of closely aggregated nanoparticles including twin nanocrystals, therefore, the additional defects such as dislocations and vacancies may form at the resultant interfaces [53]. These factors all cause the EL spectra to become larger line width and redshift relative to PL spectra.

In summary, we have investigated the fluorescence mechanism behind the amusing luminescence behaviors of the diamond QDs both experimentally and theoretically. The luminescence spectroscopy and surface structure characterization along with first principles calculation together reveal the existence of various types of luminescent oxygen (nitrogen)-related surface defects in the diamond QDs. The calculation indicates that stronger transitions between band edges of C(O)NH2-terminated CQDs leads to relatively strong photoluminescence in strong contrast with usually weak emission in oxygen terminated CQDs. Finally, the solid-state LED devices with different emission colors were fabricated based on the pH-sensitive properties of the diamond QDs.
Yuchan Wang: Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation.
Fujia Zeng: Experimental operation, Validation.
Liangrui Zhang: Methodology, Software, Validation.
Guanghao Chen: Software, Validation.
Qian Zhang: Supervision.
Nan Zhang: Resources.
Wenxia Zhang: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

Herein, we develop one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize diamond QDs, which is a special carrier for studying the luminescence mechanism on account of the characteristics of wide band gap. Significantly, the prepared diamond QDs exhibit a series of emission bands across the whole visible region, and highly pH-sensitive fluorescence characteristics. Finally, the diamond QDs were used as light-converting phosphors materials to fabricate solid-state LED. We aim to address the underlying luminescence mechanism behind this enticing phenomenon via experimental study combining with theoretical calculation.
See the supplementary material for additional optical and structural characterizations.
The manuscript was written through the contributions of all of the authors. All of the authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Scientific and Technological Research Foundation of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission under Grant (Nos. KJQN202100611 and KJQN201900630).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110343.
